Details:
Adsorption can be very effective (up to greater than 99 percent removal) and is a commonly used method for arsenic treatment. Arsenic removal by adsorptive media is largely dependent on the initial concentration, oxidation state, adsorbent type, and water chemistry (pH and competing anions). Adsorptive removal is more efficient for As(V) than for As(III), so As(III) is usually pre-oxidized to As(V) prior to adsorptive treatment. Aluminum and iron oxide/hydroxides (amorphous or granular) performed the most consistently, achieving greater than 99 percent of As(V). Adsorption on activated alumina is listed as one of the Best Available Technologies (BATs) for arsenic removal. Activated alumina may be used in either throw-away or regenerated modes.
Iron based resins, iron oxide coated sand, iron oxide impregnated activated carbon, iron oxide impregnated activated alumina, titanium based media, zirconium based media, and minerals such as goethite, magnetite also successfully removed up to 95 percent As(V) under appropriate treatment conditions.
